what_it_does: Acceptance test file that checks runtime.context_ops exports read_context, compact_session, and ContextOpError; that unreachable-supervisor calls fail loudly; and that mcp_face.tools.context registers a context tool whose dispatcher supports read/compact ops and returns structured errors rather than failing silently.
observations: Top docstring states the file tests S-R10.1 context ops + tool acceptance and explicitly notes live /context and /compact mechanics were verified by external probes, not exercised here.
Implements a minimal inline test harness with global PASS/FAIL lists and a check() helper that prints per-test results.
Defines a raises() helper that asserts a callable throws and optionally checks the exception message substring.
Uses a MockMCP class that only implements .tool() to capture the function registered by CT.register().
Tests both exception-based failure (context_ops.read_context with unreachable base) and structured-error failure (tool(op='read', session='nope')).
Asserts CT.OPS equals ('read', 'compact') to align the dispatcher with exported operation names.
Inserts the project root into sys.path so runtime and mcp_face packages are importable.
standouts: Fail-loud testing is the dominant theme: unreachable supervisor, missing session, and failed op all must produce explicit errors.
The file bridges two modules (runtime.context_ops and mcp_face.tools.context) in a single acceptance suite using a minimal mock.
Comments reference external live probes as the source of truth for /context and /compact behavior, making this file a contract test rather than an integration test.
conventions: Uses sys.path manipulation at module load time to make sibling packages importable.
Uses a hand-rolled assertion harness instead of unittest or pytest.
Defines a nested decorator function inside a method to intercept tool registration.
Follows a docstring-first convention with a detailed scope and external-verification disclaimer.
Breaks the convention of isolated unit tests by coupling runtime module tests with tool-registration tests in one file.
concerns: Global mutable PASS/FAIL lists could be affected by import side effects or repeated runs.
MockMCP only stubs .tool(), so the register() contract may be under-specified if other MCP methods are required.
Hard-coded unreachable address 127.0.0.1:1 assumes the environment has nothing listening there.
raises() returns False both when no exception is raised and when the substring does not match, making failure messages less precise.
Relies on comments claiming external lead verification; those claims are not reproducible from this file alone.
No cleanup of the inserted sys.path entry.
notes: A lightweight, hand-rolled acceptance test that verifies context-operation exports, fail-loud behavior, and MCP tool registration contract for the context tool.
questions: What is the exact interface expected by CT.register(suite=None) beyond a .tool() method?
What do the live /context and /compact commands look like, and how do the external probes validate them?
What fields does the structured error returned by tool(op='read', session='nope') contain besides ok and error?
Is the order of CT.OPS significant, or is it only a set of supported operation names?
Are PASS/FAIL consumed by an external runner, or are they only for the final printed summary?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches — the docstring says it covers the tool register contract, op dispatch, and fail-loud paths, and the code exercises exactly those areas.
apparent_themes: Acceptance-level verification over fine-grained unit testing
External verification by reference rather than live integration
intention_signals: The file appears to be trying to guarantee that context operations and their MCP tool wrapper expose a stable, observable contract: the right exports, the right dispatcher, and explicit errors on every failure path, while deferring live integration behavior to separately verified probes.
novelty: It is unusual for an acceptance test to explicitly disclaim testing the live /context and /compact mechanics and instead cite external lead-verified probes as the source of truth for those behaviors.
contribution: It contributes a concise, mock-based acceptance check that the context-ops module and its MCP tool registration surface the expected operations, dispatcher, and structured error behavior, which a fused system could keep as a quick contract guard.
summary_for_embedding: An inline acceptance test for context operation exports, dispatcher alignment, and fail-loud structured-error behavior in the context tool registration path.
intention_for_embedding: To verify the public contract and error-surfacing behavior of the context operations module and its MCP tool wrapper without exercising live Claude integration.

symbols.does: Test assertion helper that records pass/fail and prints a result line.
symbols.description: check(n, c, d='') appends the test name n to PASS if condition c is truthy and to FAIL otherwise, then prints a PASS/FAIL line with an optional detail message when failing.

symbols.does: Helper that checks whether a callable raises an exception and whether the message contains a substring.
symbols.description: raises(fn, sub='') invokes fn() inside a try/except; it returns True if an exception is raised and, when sub is provided, the exception string contains sub. It is used to assert fail-loud behavior.

symbols.does: Minimal stand-in for an MCP host that captures tool registrations.
symbols.description: MockMCP is a small class used to satisfy the register() contract just enough to capture the function passed to .tool(), storing it in a module-level captured dictionary for later assertions.

symbols.does: Method that returns a decorator for registering a tool function.
symbols.description: tool(self) returns deco, which records the registered function in captured['context'] and returns it unchanged, allowing the test to inspect the tool registered by CT.register().

symbols.does: Decorator that captures the registered tool function.
symbols.description: deco(fn) is the nested closure returned by MockMCP.tool; it stores fn under captured['context'] and returns fn, acting as the hook that lets the test retrieve the registered context tool.
